votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by converting the string to lowercase characters and using a set to count the number of distinct characters, which matches the Coq requirement of finding a duplicate-free list of the same elements with length n.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by converting the string to lowercase characters, collecting them in a set to find the unique elements, and checking if the size of the set equals n.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by converting the string to lowercase characters, finding the unique characters using a set, and checking if the number of unique characters equals n.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. ascii_lower converts uppercase ASCII letters to lowercase identically. list_of_string converts a string to a list of characters. The spec asks whether there exists a list U with no duplicates containing exactly the elements of L with length n. In Python, set(L) gives the unique elements, and checking len(set(L)) == n is equivalent to checking if the count of distinct elements equals n. The Coq spec is satisfied iff the number of distinct lowercase characters equals n, which is exactly what the Python code checks.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. ascii_lower converts uppercase A-Z to lowercase identically. list_of_string converts string to list of characters. The spec checks if there exists a list U with no duplicates containing exactly the elements of L with length n. In Python, set(L) gives the unique elements, and checking len(set(L)) == n is equivalent to checking if the number of distinct characters equals n. The Coq spec's existential quantifier over NoDup lists with the same elements as L is satisfied exactly when the count of distinct elements equals n.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. ascii_lower converts uppercase ASCII letters to lowercase identically. list_of_string converts a string to a list of characters. The spec asks whether there exists a list U with no duplicates containing exactly the elements of L with length n. In Python, set(L) gives the unique elements, and checking len(set(L)) == n is equivalent to checking if the count of distinct characters equals n. The Coq spec is satisfied iff the number of distinct lowercase-converted characters equals n, which is exactly what the Python code checks.

Both lowercase only ASCII A-Z, form the character list, and require n to equal the number of distinct elements; using a set in Python matches the Coq existence of a NoDup list with the same membership.
Both compute lowercase for ASCII A-Z, derive the set of distinct characters from the string, and check n equals its cardinality; behavior matches on all ASCII inputs.

Both compute the number of distinct characters after converting ASCII A-Z to lowercase; Python’s len(set(lowered_chars)) matches the Coq spec’s existence of a NoDup list U with the same membership and length n.
